title: Table: The table below shows the percentage use of four different fuel types to generat...
description: The table below shows the percentage use of four different fuel types to generate electricity in five Asian countries in 2005. Summarise the information by selecting, reporting the main features, and make comparison where relevant.
guide_html:
HƯỚNG DẪN VIẾT BÀI

Fuel types used to generate electricity (5 Asian countries, 2005)

1. Giải thích đề

Bảng cho thấy tỷ lệ (%) sử dụng 4 loại nhiên liệu để sản xuất điện ở 5 quốc gia châu Á năm 2005.

→ Dạng: so sánh giữa các quốc gia \+ nhiều categories

2. Introduction (paraphrase)

  • Giữ đủ: percentage – fuel types – generate electricity – five Asian countries – 2005
  • Dùng từ:
  • shows → illustrates / compares
  • use → consumption / usage

3. Overview (ý chính)

  • Không có 1 loại nhiên liệu thống trị tất cả quốc gia
  • Coal chiếm ưu thế ở Malaysia & Singapore
  • Nuclear nổi bật ở Japan & South Korea
  • Thailand phụ thuộc nhiều vào petroleum & other sources

4. Chia BODY

BODY 1 – Nhóm dùng Coal/Nuclear nhiều
  • Malaysia: coal cao nhất (61%)
  • Singapore: coal cao (42%), other cũng cao
  • Japan: nuclear cao nhất (49%)
  • South Korea: hydro \+ nuclear cao

→ Ý: các nước phát triển dùng coal/nuclear nhiều

BODY 2 – Nhóm còn lại / khác biệt
  • Thailand: không dùng nuclear (0%)
  • Petroleum cao nhất ở Thailand (36%)
  • Hydro cao nhất ở South Korea (48%)
  • Other cao ở Thailand & Singapore

→ Ý: Thailand khác biệt nhất (phụ thuộc nhiên liệu khác)

5. Gợi ý từ vựng

  • account for → chiếm
  • dominate → chiếm ưu thế
  • rely on → phụ thuộc vào
  • make up → tạo thành
  • proportion → tỷ lệ

Ví dụ:

  • Coal accounts for the largest proportion in Malaysia.
  • Thailand relies heavily on petroleum and other sources.

The table compares the proportions of electricity generated from four fuel types in five Asian countries in 2005.

Overall, coal and lignite dominated electricity production in Malaysia and Singapore, while nuclear power was the main source in Japan and South Korea. By contrast, Thailand relied heavily on petroleum products and other sources, with no use of nuclear energy.

In Malaysia, coal and lignite accounted for the majority at 61%, followed by nuclear energy at 19%, while the remaining sources made relatively small contributions. A similar pattern can be seen in Singapore, where coal (42%) was the largest source, although a substantial share also came from other sources (39%). In contrast, Japan depended primarily on nuclear power, which made up 49% of electricity generation, with other sources contributing 39%, while coal, petroleum and hydro played only minor roles.

South Korea also relied heavily on nuclear energy (33%) and hydro and wind (48%), with very limited use of coal and petroleum. Thailand showed a markedly different profile, as it generated most of its electricity from other sources (42%) and petroleum products (36%), alongside a notable proportion from hydro and wind (19%), but no nuclear power at all.
